data.title: Energy And The AI Buildout: An Investor's Perspective
data.description:
By Tony Kim & Tomas Hamudis

Scarce Today, Abundant Tomorrow: Power and the AI Buildout

We are in the middle of what could be a once-in-a-generation industrial buildout. It requires semiconductors, equipment, labor, data centers, and massive amounts of power. Power is one of the bottlenecks, but the system is adapting across the short and long term. We are building the physical foundation for scaling machine intelligence and accelerating the next wave of productivity growth.

What Is the Demand From Data Centers?

We expect approximately 148GW of additional power capacity will be needed by the end of the decade to satisfy data center demand, more than quadrupling the roughly 42GW in power capacity consumed by data centers in 2025. Including non-data center load growth, we expect power demand to rise by 23GW in 2026 and 43GW in 2027.

Data centers to require 148GW of additional capacity this decade

Forecasted U.S. Power Demand

How Did We Get Here?

Since the launch of ChatGPT in late 2022, AI compute investments have risen massively, tracking to exceed $1T per year. Even so, demand for compute has been systematically underestimated. As AI evolves from simple queries to multimodal systems and persistent agents, across more users, and with longer runtimes, compute demand compounds. Efficiency gains have lowered unit costs, but aggregate demand has scaled even faster, making electricity a key factor in AI deployment.

What is the Urgency?

We believe the economics are decisive. Our research shows that a 1GW AI data center can cost roughly $40B to build, including equipment such as GPUs and networking systems, yet can possibly generate more than $12B of annual high margin revenue almost immediately. Power is the gate to that cash flow. But the urgency is not only economic. AI is also a strategic priority. In this environment, power develops a yield curve: capacity available today could be worth far more than capacity delivered years from now.

What is the Power Bottleneck?

Current indications suggest that the system is nearly sold out. There is very little incremental AI power capacity available over the next two years beyond what is already under construction or in advanced development. Grid interconnection queues are congested. Gas turbine deliveries extend into 2030. Transformers and switchgear are in short supply. Engineering, procurement, and construction crews are in high demand.

Will Power Constraints Stop the AI Data Center Buildout?

Based on our comprehensive review of ongoing and future power supply needs for the AI data center buildout, the answer is no. Near-term constraints are being addressed through pragmatic solutions. In the out years of 2029-2030 we see incentives and innovation as likely combining to meet modest shortfalls. The system is adjusting across multiple time horizons, solving for immediate gaps while expanding the structural base.

A Collision Between Two Worlds: Tech and Power

The increasing use of power to build AI Infrastructure has forced a collision between two radically different operating cultures.

  • The AI ecosystem, including labs, hyperscalers, and semiconductor companies, moves quickly, with competitive and financial incentives for rapid iteration, aggressive capital deployment and first-mover advantage.
  • The power complex, including utilities, grid operators, regional markets, and equipment manufacturers, has seen little structural growth for nearly two decades and is optimized for reliability, regulatory compliance, capital preservation, and risk minimization.

When unprecedented demand collides with slow-moving, risk-averse institutions, strain is inevitable. The response is creativity. The AI ecosystem is shifting to new geographies, underwriting long-term contracts, and pushing supply chains to expand. Developers are also reducing grid dependence by building generation alongside compute, a trend known as “Behind the Meter” or “Bring Your Own Power (BYOP)”. Projects launch with anchor capacity and scale in phases, accelerating capacity beyond what the grid can deliver.

Case Studies: New Models for Powering AI

xAI in Memphis, Tennessee

xAI built one of the largest AI compute clusters in the world, deploying 100,000 GPUs in roughly 122 days. The site operates at close to 1GW of power capacity, comparable to the electricity demand of a major city. Faced with multi-year grid interconnection timelines, xAI moved quickly, deploying road portable natural gas turbines under temporary permits. The site integrates batteries, mobile generation, and grid supply in a layered system designed for load variability. Launch fast, then optimize.

Stargate in Abilene, Texas

The data center campus in Abilene, Texas, part of the Stargate project and developed with Crusoe, reflects the next phase of large-scale AI infrastructure. The campus was developed with a 1.2GW grid interconnection, behind-the-meter energy resources, including natural gas turbines to support on-site generation. The campus is now live, with expansion underway toward its planned 1.2GW of total capacity. West Texas offers abundant land, existing energy infrastructure, and a supportive regulatory environment. This is not only bridge power, it is vertically integrated infrastructure purpose built for AI.

Case studies are shown for illustrative and educational purposes only. They do not represent BlackRock investments or strategies and are not a prediction of future results. Outcomes may differ materially depending on market, regulatory, and operational factors. References to specific companies are for illustrative purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or investment recommendations.

Can the System Support the Current View on Demand?

In the near term, yes. Based on our exhaustive analysis, we conclude that the power supply systems fueling AI are more flexible than commonly assumed. Gas turbine manufacturing is already scaling and can accelerate further, solar is growing rapidly, and emerging technologies such as fuel cells are contributing. Batteries play a growing and meaningful role by raising the yield of power-generating sources. Through 2026–2027, available capacity, ongoing buildouts, and interim solutions appear sufficient. After 2027, the system is more stretched, but uncertainty on both sides is higher.

Power pressures grow, but solutions emerging

US Power Surplus

Power supply systems fueling AI are more flexible than assumed

Forecasted U.S. Power Supply

  • Natural gas is the primary source of incremental AI-grade power over the next several years, particularly in regions with existing pipeline infrastructure. In 2026–2027, supply may come from higher utilization of the existing fleet, slower retirements, and increasingly net new gas additions. New-build gas capacity could ramp more materially in 2027–2028 and beyond as turbine deliveries accelerate. Turbine manufacturers are expanding capacity, supported by U.S. policy aimed at increasing output, and we see upside to current production plans. We expect approximately +20GW of net new gas capacity in 2026–2027 and +61GW through the end of the decade.
  • Solar is large, growing rapidly, and highly cost-competitive. It can be deployed faster than gas in many instances and, given intermittency, is deployed as part of the grid or with battery storage systems. Global manufacturing capacity is ample, albeit concentrated in Asia. We expect approximately +14GW of net new solar capacity in 2026–2027 and +35GW through 2030, with additional upside if demand accelerates.
  • Wind adds utility-scale, cost-competitive generation in high-resource regions with existing transmission. While development timelines can be longer than solar, wind complements solar output and improves overall grid reliability. We expect approximately +6GW in 2026–2027 and +14GW through 2030.
  • Batteries (stationary storage) expand power supply by improving utilization of existing assets. Storage enables balancing and peak support, allowing operators to defer or avoid other investments. Storage is quick to deploy and is a critical tool supporting demand growth. We estimate batteries can support approximately +9GW in 2026–2027 and +27GW through 2030.
  • Bring Your Own Power (BYOP) has emerged as a source of incremental supply that did not exist at scale 18 months ago. Some projects are true standalone power islands using large gas turbines, while turbine scarcity has driven modular solutions including smaller gas turbines, reciprocating engines, batteries, and fuel cells. Together, these approaches have created a new layer of supply that can be brought online faster than conventional grid-connected generation. We estimate BYOP deployments can support approximately +5GW in 2026–2027 and +20GW through the end of the decade, with upside if grid constraints persist and demand exceeds expectations.
  • Coal retirement schedules are not immutable. When grid reliability is at stake, timelines can shift, as recent emergency orders delaying plant closures amid rising data center load demonstrate. Existing coal plants represent supply that can remain online for years if economics and policy align. We expect retirements of 5GW net capacity in 2026–2027 and 20GW through the end of the decade.

BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026. Chart shows power generation net capacity broken down by fuel source (Nuclear, Wind, Bring-Your-Own-Power, Battery, Solar, Gas, and Coal + Other).

How Is the Ecosystem Adapting?

Generation is only part of the adjustment. The AI buildout is reshaping geography, capital flows, infrastructure design, and policy.

  • Location, location, location? Historically, data centers clustered near population centers to minimize latency. Today, much of the incremental build, particularly large-scale AI training clusters, is moving toward power-rich regions such as West Texas, where large blocks of capacity can be secured and developed faster.
  • Capital is underwriting supply. Hyperscalers are no longer passive buyers of electricity. Through long-dated contracts and development partnerships, they are anchoring new generation projects and accelerating capacity.
  • Stranded infrastructure is being repriced. Decommissioned power plants, idle industrial sites, and crypto mining facilities with existing transmission or fuel access are now among the fastest paths to incremental AI-grade power.
  • Policy is compressing timelines and expanding supply. AI is now a strategic priority. Regulators are rethinking interconnection processes and ensuring hyperscalers fund infrastructure. Governments are also pushing manufacturers of turbines and other critical equipment to expand production capacity.

What Long-Term Solutions Could Expand Supply in the 2030s?

As we move into the 2030s, two developments have the potential to materially change how AI infrastructure is powered and scaled.

  • Nuclear energy is one of the few clean, always-on sources capable of supporting large, concentrated AI loads. Hyperscalers are already contracting for output tied to extending, refurbishing, or restarting existing plants. Small modular reactors (SMRs) offer a model that could be deployed closer to data centers. Sustained AI demand is increasing political and financial support for new-build nuclear, positioning nuclear as a critical pillar of AI power beyond 2030.
  • Data centers in space are moving from theory to exploration. SpaceX has proposed orbital data centers powered by continuous solar exposure, leveraging natural cooling and eliminating terrestrial grid constraints. Google, Amazon, and others are evaluating similar concepts. With reusable launch systems reducing launch costs and increasing payload capacity, deploying large-scale orbital compute over time is technically plausible, introducing an entirely new supply frontier.

The Bigger Prize

Every megawatt unlocked expands our capacity to scale machine intelligence. This is not simply an infrastructure story. It is a productivity story. For most of modern history, labor has accounted for more than half of global GDP. AI has the potential to alter the relationship between labor, capital, and output itself. That is why the power constraint should be solved. The prize is too consequential and too strategically visible for it not to be.

Conclusion

Power constraints are unlikely to derail AI data center deployment on their own. Over the next two years, the gap is bridged by a mix of in-flight capacity, gas-led additions, storage, BYOP, and site re-optimization. Beyond 2027, the challenge becomes less about “is there enough power?” and more about how quickly the system can translate capital into energized capacity.

Importantly, these frictions create a self-regulating mechanism: they cap the pace of expansion, prioritize the highest-return projects, and reduce the risk of indiscriminate overbuilding.

For investors, that means the opportunity is shifting from a broad AI rally to more selective stock exposure. In an environment with dynamic bottlenecks and timelines, dispersion should rise, and we believe an active, nimble approach is best positioned to capture the winners.

Sources

[Power Supply Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026. Chart shows power generation net capacity broken down by fuel source (Nuclear, Wind, Bring-Your-Own-Power, Battery, Solar, Gas, and Coal + Other).

[Power Demand Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from US Energy Information Administration as of October 16, 2025 and from Semianalysis as of January 12, 2026. Chart shows US power demand by sector (Data center and Other).

[Power Surplus Chart] Source: BlackRock Fundamental Equities, with data from Bloomberg New Energy Finance and US Energy Information Administration as of February 24, 2026, and Semianalysis as of January 12, 2026. Chart shows surplus/deficit of US net power generation capacity in excess of US power demand.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple: I See An Alchain-Allen Theorem At Play (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis review

The hardware and electronics market is facing a margin headwind that's being caused by the supply shortage of DRAM and NAND. This shortage is estimated to persist for the duration of 2026 and maybe last until 2027. This headwind has painted a bad margin picture for firms that have memory as one of their input costs; Dell (DELL), Xiaomi (XIACY), and Apple (AAPL) are a few of the names expected to experience lower gross margins in the subsequent quarters. As a result, I figured it would be prudent to review my initial thesis on Apple. In my previous article, I was refuting the ongoing bear thesis that said iPhone sales declines will result in Apple's valuation contracting. My rebuttal was that given the revenue growth being witnessed in the services segment, Apple's bottom line will grow even higher, sustaining shareholder yield. Since the publication of my article, the stock has moved in line with the broader market.

Even looking at the YoY return, investors would have been better off holding the (SPY) than the Apple stock when adjusting for the concentration risk. This performance is simply disappointing for me and illustrative of the fact that Apple currently trades at fair value and the market doesn't see further upside. Meanwhile, underneath, I see a growth catalyst that makes me even more bullish on the name.

Bull Case

Contrary to the popular view, I actually see the memory shortage as a growth catalyst for Apple. The reason being, the shortage is going to push other hardware providers (with less pricing power) to increase prices to their consumers, reducing the price differential between standard and premium hardware (Apple products); this reduction will create a higher demand for Apple's products as the quality of the products will now be more attractive. This phenomenon is called the Alchian-Allen theorem, and we are already seeing signs of it at play. In Q1 of 2026, Apple led the global shipment of smartphones (5% YoY growth) while the overall market declined, with Xiaomi recording one of the highest drops of 19% YoY. During the Q1 earnings call, the analyst from TD Cowen asked if it was possible that the record numbers that were hit in Q1 were due to the memory constraints, and from Tim Cook's response, it's likely that is what we are witnessing.

The market hasn't fully priced this growth catalyst in, and this is reflected by forward revenue estimates marking lower than YoY growth. I agree that we are likely to see a minor margin hit in the coming quarters, but topline growth will likely top the current estimates as the install base continues to hit record numbers across all geographies. I now feel confident to upgrade Apple to a Strong Buy.

Momentum

As I pointed out earlier, Apple's performance hasn't been that impressive; over the period of 3 years, the stock only appreciated by 63.5%, while its peers grew by triple digits and the SPY grew by 72%. YTD the stock is still trailing at -0.6%, while the SPY is up by more than 4%. I think the stock simply needs a growth catalyst that will validate a premium valuation, and I think we are now at that inflection point.

Financial Analysis

Q1 2026 Income Statement

Looking at the last quarter's earnings results, Apple's revenue grew 16% to $143.76 billion, beating estimates by $5.23 billion. The last time Apple had an estimate beat of such magnitude was in Q1 of 2021. The growth was led by iPhone sales growing 23% to $85.27 billion, followed by services growing 14% to $30.01 billion. Mac sales offset growth with a 7% decline to $8.60 billion. Cost of revenue grew 13% to $74.53 billion; the growth was led by the cost of product increasing 14% to $67.48 billion, while the cost of services increased by only 7% to $7.05 billion. This resulted in gross profits increasing 19% to $69.23 billion and marking gross margins of 48.16%. Product gross margins increased 450 bps QoQ to 40.7%, while services gross margins increased 120 bps QoQ to 76.5%, both driven by favorable mix and leverage. Operating expenses increased 19% to $18.38 billion, driven by a significant growth in R&D spending to the value of $10.89 billion. This led to operating income growing 19% to $50.85 billion and pretax income growing 20% to $51.01 billion. Income tax increased by 42%, resulting in net income increasing 16% to $42.10 billion and GAAP EPS increasing to $2.84, beating estimates by $0.17.

Apple's margin profile signifies the firm's pricing power in relation to its peers. The scatterplot below shows how Apple's margins are far above its industry peers in the electronics and hardware space. Apple's gross margin slightly trails the sector margin of 49.98%; however, it significantly exceeds the sector net margins of 5.27%.

Q1 Cash Flow Statement

Operating cash flow grew 80% to $53.93 billion, with the growth bolstered by a cash inflow of $5.55 billion from net working capital. Free cash flow grew 90% to $47.96 billion, driven by CAPEX shrinking 19% to $2.37 billion and offset by SBC increasing 9% to $3.59 billion.

With EBIT margins of 32.28%, Apple exceeds its sector margin of 6.81% and peers like Dell, with an EBIT margin of 7.68%, and Xiaomi, with an EBIT margin of 7.49%. FCF margins are also impressive at 24.41% vs. the sector margin of 11.14%. These incredible profitability metrics have resulted in the Quant system granting it a profitability grade of A+. Given the low-margin profile Apple peers have, the memory shortage will likely corner these firms to pass through the rising input costs to their end consumers, inevitably increasing the demand for Apple's products.

Q1 2026 Balance Sheet

Apple's balance sheet remains in great condition, with cash reserves increasing 50% to $45.3 billion while inventories decreased 15% to $5.9 billion. This significant decrease in inventories is also illustrative of the increasing demand for Apple's products but also shows the ongoing shortage in input components and supply chain disruptions. Current debt increased 9% to $11.8 billion, while long-term debt decreased 9% to $76.7 billion, landing total debt at $91 billion and debt to FCF at 1.67. Apple is a cash-generative powerhouse, and the short-term margin compression shows little threat to the firm's shareholder yield.

Valuation

The Seeking Quant system gave Apple a valuation grade of F, implying a premium valuation, but this is mostly driven by the price-to-book multiple, which is slightly misleading as book value doesn't accurately reflect Apple's earnings growth. Looking at other multiples, we can see that Apple trades in line with its peers and sector. Apple has an EBITDA multiple of 25.39 and a cash flow multiple of 29.28, slightly above the sector EBITDA multiple of 19.15 and a cash flow multiple of 18.76. The chart below also shows Apple slightly positioned lower than a trendline, implying the cash flow multiple could be lower than it's deserved.

Analyzing the valuation beyond its industry peers, I made a screen of all tech stocks that trade for a PE ranging between 30 and 50, and then I made a scatter plot that compares the FCF multiple vs. operating margin. The results below show that Apple still maintains higher operating margins than most names in this basket; however, the FCF multiple is in line with the trend at 32.50x (also in line with its 3-year rolling median). This solidifies the point that Apple looks fairly valued, and given the ongoing weak momentum, the market doesn't see further upside.

Q2 Outlook

I strongly believe that topline growth, especially coming from the product segment, will push multiples to the premium level, a well-deserved valuation for a brand name like Apple with a monopolistic position and a high margin profile. While the memory shortage didn't compress gross margins in Q1, management stated that the impact will be slightly higher in Q2 but still guided for a margin range of 48%-49%, implying that topline growth and the services segment will largely offset the margin compression from the product side. The revenue guide ranged from 13% to 16%, with constraints in iPhone supply priced in. In a week from now, Apple is going to release Q2 earnings results, I suggest investors pay attention to the product segment growth, as this is the make-or-break point of my strong buy thesis. The growth in product sales would be validating my high-demand expectation from end consumers.

Risk

Looking at the risk profile, I want to start by saying that upgrading Apple to a strong buy is a highly speculative take, as this name is not insulated from the memory shortage headwind. Just because there could be a growing demand for Apple's products, this doesn't imply the demand will immediately translate to sales, as we all know that for that to happen, the demand must be met with adequate supply. This will simply not happen if the impact of memory shortage is significantly higher than my expectations. Another risk is that in recent news, Tim Cook is expected to step down as Apple's CEO in H2 of 2026. This adds a management risk that could impact Apple stock; in fact, after the news broke, the stock fell by 0.8%.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I'm upgrading Apple to a strong buy, as I see the shortage in memory could catalyze the growth of the product segment. This is the business segment that has received a healthy amount of criticism and laid a bear case for the stock in recent years. While I don't see higher margin contributions in the short term, the growth will eventually be margin accretive as the product segment serves as an onboarding mechanism to more service growth, the segment that has higher margins. Even though we might not see margin growth in 2026, I expect to see it once the memory shortage issue has been resolved, which is estimated to be around 2027.

data.title: Equity LifeStyle Properties: Fairly Valued Given Seasonal Pressures
data.description:
Shares of Equity Lifestyle Properties (ELS) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, trading essentially flat and missing out on a meaningful equity market rally. The stock has been held back by a full valuation and pressure in its seasonal business, especially with less travel from Canadians into the United States. That said, the company reported solid Q1 results, and some of its cost pressures may be fading. I last covered shares in December, where I rated the stock a suitable “Hold” in portfolios but held back from a "Buy" rating. With a 7% return since then, that call was overly cautious, and a "Buy" was merited. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s first quarter, Equity Lifestyle generated $0.84 of funds from operations, which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew 3% to $398 million. Core FFO was up just 0.3% from last year as higher interest expenses largely offset a 1.8% increase in EBITDA to $201 million. Core property revenue grew 3.7%, while expenses increased 4.9%, but I expect cost inflation to improve over the course of the year.

While mobile homes are less than 76,000 of its 173,000 sites, they do drive the majority of the business, at about 63% of revenue. 91% of ELS’s revenue is annual, leading to steady cash flow, though seasonal activity drives near-term variances. The company operates across 35 states, but Florida, California, and Arizona are the majority of its business, with Florida over a third all by itself. These Sun Belt markets have enjoyed meaningful domestic migration as well as an influx of retirees. These trends have supported demand for mobile homes, especially with lower initial ownership costs than a single-family home, making them attractive for retirees in particular.

In its mobile home unit, base rental income was up a solid 5.7% from last year. Units were up about 0.5% from last year to 73,600, and the company continues to pass on rent increases to tenants. Rents were up about 6% to $948/month. However, occupancy was down 60 bps from last year. ELS has consistently increased rent, and while mobile homes are still much more affordable than single-family homes, this may be starting to limit demand, especially as migration to Florida has slowed.

Aside from this, its RV and Marina unit continues to struggle, and an unseasonably cold winter across the Southeast was a further headwind. Core income declined by 1.4% from last year to $114.5 million. Annual sales were up 4% to $80 million. However, seasonal activity fell by 15% to $23 million, with transient activity down 7%. While annual activity provides a significant base of revenue, seasonal activity is more economically sensitive and continues to be weak. Seasonal activity has generally been soft from less Canadian tourism, and I expect that to remain the case given the souring of geopolitical relations.

Additionally, higher fuel costs, given the war in Iran, may further reduce discretionary travel and vacations. While not an impact on the annual business, it can make for further weakness in seasonal activity, and as such, I see risks of further weakness. Fuel economy can be 8-22 mpg, a relatively low level, so higher prices, combined with ongoing affordability challenges, may reduce revenue. I continue to believe that markets are underpricing the risk from the Strait of Hormuz closure and lost oil supply.

Importantly, ELS can make up some of this pressure on the expense side. On April 1, it renewed its insurance program and saw an 18% decrease in premiums. Thanks to legislative changes in Florida and a quiet hurricane season in 2025, we have seen meaningful disinflation in insurance costs after significant increases post-2020. This should help to drive expenses lower over the balance of the year. Insurance costs were flat in Q1 at $27 million, and they should run below $25 million over the rest of 2026. This saving can help to offset the weakness in the RV and marina unit.

One risk to be mindful of is utilities account for about 28% of expenses, and only half of this is recovered, leaving ~14% of expenses as an ELS exposure. Utility prices have risen over the past few years, and there are concerns that incremental data center demand could raise electricity prices further. This remains to be seen, and more recently, natural gas prices have come down, which should alleviate some of the upward pressure on utility costs. Given this, I do not see utility costs being an upside risk in 2026, though some longer-term inflation is possible.

Equity Lifestyle maintains a solid balance sheet with $3.3 billion of debt. Debt/EBITDA ended the quarter at 4.5x, flat sequentially and up just slightly from 4.4x a year ago. I like to see leverage below 5x, and ELS is comfortably below this level, providing plenty of headroom for bolt-on M&A should opportunities arise. Plus, it has no maturities in 2026 and just $200 million next year, limiting its near-term sensitivity to interest rates. Given the health of its balance sheet, I view its dividend as secure, and shares offer a 3.3% dividend yield.

Alongside results, ELS reaffirmed full-year core FFO guidance of $3.12-$3.22. However, it reduced core growth rates a bit, reflecting ongoing pressure in seasonal activity. Its mobile home forecast is unchanged at 5.6%, but RV and marina are expected to grow just 2.4% from 2.9%. Fortunately, it can offset this with lower property expenses. Back in December, I was targeting at least $3.20 in 2026 FFO, so the company’s outlook is not that different from my own. I now see results in the $3.16-$3.22 area, given some continued sluggishness in seasonal demand.

As has been the case for some time, Equity Lifestyle scores poorly on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener. Now, the real estate sector faces many challenges, and while not perfect, I view ELS’s fundamentals as being stronger than many subsectors. For instance, office and mall real estate face significant secular pressure and lower multiples as a result. While the multifamily sector has strong long-term fundamentals in my view, apartment rental inflation is currently fragile given a surge of construction activity post-COVID. In contrast, mobile home rental inflation remains solidly positive and does not face secular decline. ELS also maintains a conservative balance sheet.

In my view, these factors merit a premium valuation to the sector, and I would not "Sell" ELS simply because it trades at a premium to the real estate sector. I expect this premium to be fairly structural in nature. Shares offer a ~4.9% FFO yield, and I see about 3%-4% long-term growth given modest unit count growth and above-inflation rent increases. As such, ELS can provide up to an ~8% long-term return, especially as its balance sheet provides flexibility for M&A, but I struggle to see much more than that.

Over time, that positions ELS to be a market performer rather than an outperformer, in my view. As such, I continue to see shares as a suitable "Hold" in portfolios, but I see no rush to "Buy." Given its largely annual revenue stream and normalizing insurance costs, I am not a seller, and ELS provides a sustainable combination of income and income growth. That said, I would want to see shares retrace towards $60 before buying more and would look elsewhere for better upside opportunities.

data.title: Micron: A Study Of Event Returns, Regime Tailwinds, And DRAM ASP
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Scott Gossett as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Intro & Thesis

Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) is a buy-the-dip and hold for a 1-year horizon. This thesis partly involves a study behind the volatility post-earnings, which were recently released and outperformed exceptionally. However, a negative reaction followed in the stock price. This study demonstrates that the negative reaction was a characteristic pattern of Micron post-earnings dates over the past two years, and my thesis is also that it was exemplified by the negative stock market catalyst of the Iran war.

The rating for this asset is a buy. The valuation one year out is approximately $900 at an 8x multiple. The recent run in Micron stock was aligned with top-down tailwinds, which included the second round of Fed interest rate easing and DRAM average selling price surpassing post-Covid peak highs and entering a breakout phase. This was translated through exemplary operating fundamentals, shown quarterly as improving operating leverage, cash flow, and guidance.

Company & Business Overview

Micron is in the business of manufacturing and selling memory as a fab operating semiconductor manufacturer. The two forms of memory are NAND and DRAM. Proportionally, DRAM makes up the majority of their sales revenue and is their primary revenue driver. Their chief competitors are Samsung and SK Hynix. After Covid, there was a boom in demand for memory due to a structural shift in personal computing needs. As memory prices contracted cyclically from that level, there wasn’t an incentive for memory producers to expand capacity. This has since led to a supply glut, now that AI infrastructure is receiving rapid and substantial investment, and DRAM memory is required in alignment with GPU processors. To catch up with demand, memory producers are investing to expand capacity, and given the prior cyclicality of its market, a topic of contention is the cyclicality of memory pricing, where we are in the cycle, and what the future of the cycle looks like. This article illustrates the behavior of Micron’s pricing around earnings events, a proxy of the pricing cycle for Micron’s DRAM average selling price mix, and how the stock price reacted in congruence with improving operating fundamentals and macro tailwinds.

Study Purpose

I built this study to answer a simple question: why did Micron trade poorly after what looked like outstanding earnings, and was that weakness actually the beginning of a reversal or just part of the stock’s normal behavior? To answer that, I analyzed two years of MU price behavior around catalyst events. Those catalysts included earnings dates, guidance updates, peer earnings, and guidance, HBM and DRAM pricing updates, and federal and regulatory policy events.

Regime Variables

I then layered regimes onto that event set. Those regimes included Fed rate cuts, tariff events, and a transcript-inferred DRAM pricing proxy built from Micron’s own stated pricing and bit-shipment changes over the past 20 quarters. The purpose was to identify when MU was simply moving with semis, when it was actually showing stock-specific outperformance, and whether we can identify patterns tied to these price drivers.

Cyclical Memory Pricing Proxy:

The DRAM proxy was important to this framework because I wanted a visual time series for Micron’s main revenue driver rather than using private commentary as an assumption regarding “the memory cycle” while lacking a reference point. The most relevant data isn’t easy to access, as it’s located behind paywalls. So I built a directional proxy to track Micron’s realized DRAM economics over time to serve as a regime overlay with the stock. I started with Micron’s DRAM revenue at the publicly observed DRAM cycle peak in late 2021, then rolled that revenue forward each quarter using Micron’s stated DRAM pricing change and bit-shipment change from quarterly transcripts. I lagged pricing change by one quarter in the assumption that there’s a lag in contract pricing. After roughly 20 quarters, the implied DRAM revenue came within about 2.5% of current reported DRAM revenue, which was enough for me to treat it as a useful directional proxy.

Return Pathing Analysis

From there, I looked at two types of return behavior. First, I analyzed MU’s own lead-up and post-event pathing. Second, I analyzed MU’s excess return versus iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) to isolate stock-specific behavior relative to industry/beta. That distinction matters because the same event can produce a move in MU that isn’t unique if SOXX is directionally equivalent.

The first identified pattern was that positive Micron earnings often showed strong lead-up behavior but weak short-term follow-through after the event.

Median returns around positive earnings releases showed pre-event accumulation, then negative reaction and continued deterioration through the 10-day forward window. The latest post earnings weakness coincides with this evidence. It fits a pattern that already existed in the stock. What made this decline more notable, in my view, was that an additional negative catalyst, the Iran war, exacerbated its decline.

Top-Down/Fundamental Analysis

The next question was whether the broader backdrop supported MU. I think the evidence says yes. When I overlaid Fed cut regimes, tariff events, and the DRAM pricing proxy on MU’s price path, the key contrast was that Fed Cut Regime 1 and Fed Cut Regime 2 did not behave the same. The first FED easing window occurred while both earnings and DRAM pricing were in a cyclical uptrend from lows. The second easing window occurred after earnings, margins, and memory pricing had improved materially. I believe that is why MU’s relative performance versus SOXX diverged and outperformed during the second rate cut regime, supporting that rate cuts alone weren’t the story. It was a coincidence of easier policy combined with memory reaching a cyclical price peak, demonstrated operating leverage, and strong earnings sentiment.

Micron Performance versus Industry Benchmark

The regime table illustrates this point as well. When macro support, memory backdrop (qualitative earnings performance/sentiment labeling), and DRAM pricing state were all aligned, MU’s excess returns were stronger and more consistent.

During the earlier cut regime, MU behaved much closer to the industry benchmark. The latter cut regime, however, as DRAM economics improved and operating performance/leverage strengthened, MU began to materially outperform SOXX. That evidence gave a much better explanation for the stock’s move than simply saying the second round of rate cuts explained the outperforming returns.

DRAM ASP Cycle Comparison

The DRAM side is where I think the broader message strengthens. The proxy isn’t meant to prove an exact market price for memory, but it does serve as a per-unit weighted average for their Average Selling Price (ASP). What it shows directionally is that Micron’s strongest stock phase occurs as DRAM ASP recovers from the cyclical trough, approaches prior highs, then moves through them. It’s more consistent with ongoing expansion than with a cyclical peak. It isn’t proof of permanent structural change, but it does support the view that the current memory prices surpass cyclical peak levels while MU is capitalizing constructively on their mix.

Industry Catalysts

AI-related infrastructure spending matters too. Continued CapEx by hyperscalers supports mid-term demand for AI-related memory, the higher-margin portion of Micron’s DRAM mix. This doesn’t guarantee sustained DRAM ASP levels, but it explains the stronger pricing and supports the argument that, while capex levels maintain or grow, DRAM pricing, Micron’s revenue driver, will maintain or grow. This validates Micron’s sector outperformance.

Furthermore, Micron’s capex isn’t an expected headwind since its capex-to-revenue rate has stayed within the same range for 3 years, as shown in the earlier table, and is currently at the low end of that range as of the most recent transcript. This weakens the argument for a justified selloff due to excessive capex spending.

Valuation

The below table is a forward valuation for MU 12 months out using an 8x forward multiple. The basis is rooted in revenue projections. Pricing and bit-growth assumptions are taken from TrendForce outlooks for 2026, 2027, and 2028, along with Micron’s most recent transcript.

Micron stated that additional capacity, in the formal sense, will not be online until the second half of 2027, so bit-growth assumptions in the near term are based on node migration and mix shifts. DRAM and NAND pricing are assumed to remain strong through 2026, peak in 2027, and flatten in 2028 as supply begins to normalize.

Additional assumptions include a 15% tax rate given higher profitability. The prior quarter’s tax rate was 10%. Upcoming margin guidance was used, and it is assumed that no improvement in margins occurs after this quarter, with margins instead declining gradually to reflect additional costs associated with increased capacity.

Under these mid-to-conservative assumptions, applying an 8x forward multiple justifies a $900 share price in 12 months.

Risks to Thesis

Risks to the broad mid-term setup for Micron stock are unfavorable Treasury-rate movement, a material decrease in hyperscaler CapEx spending, material deterioration or reversal of operating leverage or margins, CapEx moving outside the range of recent historical CapEx-to-revenue rates, inefficiencies, bottlenecks, or obstacles in delivering or executing expected capacity additions, a marked decline in DRAM pricing economics driving a reversal in the revenue driver, material market-share gains from Micron’s top competitors, hawkish interest-rate policy, and technological advancements resulting in a decrease in expected demand for HBM in conjunction with GPUs.

Additionally, if bit-shipment increases in the near term from node transition and improving wafer yields, and in the extended term from capacity coming online, do not offset projected stagnating revenue-driver equilibrium price growth, the setup would weaken.

To put it more concisely, the main risks are a change in the supply-demand balance of Micron’s revenue driver, poor operating execution relative to its historical norm, competitors taking market share, declines in total accessible market (TAM) expectation, or unfavorable interest rates leading to a perception or realization of decreased infrastructure investment.

Overall, I do not feel that these individual risks are within the main scope or purpose of this article’s research. However, there are market dynamics worth being mindful of, because they are the inverse of the favorable tailwinds that generated the positive idiosyncratic returns Micron has experienced since roughly the beginning of the second dovish interest-rate regime. Therefore, monitoring these risks is prudent for investors.

Conclusion

Micron’s recent weakness was an exaggerated move that was expected based on 2 years of observed forward-return pathing after positive earnings events and was further exacerbated by the Iran war catalyst. The medium-term outlook for MU indicates no break in the underlying setup, given the observed price reaction to margin-driven operating leverage, revenue-driver levels (DRAM ASP) in a breakout phase, favorable interest-rate policy, and continued stimulus to AI infrastructure through HPC capex spending. Based on the valuation metrics provided earlier, the anticipated price level for MU over the next 12 months is approximately $900 at an 8x forward multiple.

data.title: Pampa Energía: No New Catalysts, But Potential In Prices
data.description:
Since I wrote my last article about Pampa Energía (PAM) on January 12, the stock has performed broadly in line with the S&P 500 (SPY). My rating was Hold because I thought the market was already pricing in its long-term potential. I still believe its shares are priced at an entry premium, but now I believe it's time to buy before the stock breaks its all-time highs again. I see Pampa as a great opportunity to participate in Argentina's energy sector boom.

In the mentioned article, I explained the company's operations and its strength in gas and oil as well as in electricity generation. But I focused more on explaining the weight of its sales based on crude oil combined with an analysis of the strong oil inventory I was seeing at the beginning of 2026.

This led me to some estimates that were quite wrong regarding the possible price of crude oil. These were not random estimates. I based my thesis on EIA reports that predicted an average price for Q1 2026 of $50-$59. The conflict in the Middle East changed the context of the energy markets, and now the expected average price is $96, based on their latest report. The estimated average price in March was $79, a 22% difference, which shows the current uncertainty of markets. Meanwhile, I also checked future crude oil prices according to CME Group, and in Q4 2026 it drops to less than $75.

However, my analysis was thought out for the long term. I mean, in the short term, I guess I was wrong. But in the long term, I believe that oversupply and overstocking will continue to exist. In the end, my long-term thesis didn't change, although a big part of the energy infrastructure in the Middle East was damaged. What I'm trying to say is, ultimately, the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz wasn't just a logistical problem, but the damaged infrastructure could raise crude oil prices somewhat in the long term compared to prewar estimates. But the structural situation of long-term reserves is comfortable for the world economy.

Following the EIA reports, the base scenario projects a recovery of the surplus for the third quarter of the year, and 2026 would end up with a total increase in average supply of 300 kb/d. In the case of a more difficult scenario, the deficit at the end of the year would be approximately 4 to 5 mb/d (chart at the left). That would be, for me, the worst-case scenario, and it would cause prices to remain much closer to $100 than $70 for a much longer period. However, I believe that reserves remain strong enough to maintain the supply of crude oil intact. And although the infrastructure and distribution suffered serious damage, I think the balance against demand would come out more than well in the base scenario.

The Weight of Crude Oil in Pampa: Why Didn't the Stock Rise With the Supply Cuts?

Beyond the analysis of the crude oil market, I believe the important thing is to understand that Pampa's role remains key in Argentina's energy sector. In my last article, I told you I was very excited for Pampa's investments in Vaca Muerta and calculated that a crude oil price below $55-$60 could put pressure on the company's profitability in the medium term. Now, those prices are very far away due to the conflict in the Middle East.

However, Pampa's shares did not experience an extraordinary rise as a result of the increase in oil prices, which surpassed $100 on several occasions. For example, in companies operating in Vaca Muerta, such as Vista Energy (VIST), the stock rose 33% in 2026. Pampa, on the other hand, has fallen 8% so far in 2026.

I believe this is due, in part, to the fact that VIST has oil at the core of its business, while Pampa has more diversified income, with more weight in gas production and electricity generation. That's what I found in the annual reports. Pampa had sales of $2 billion in 2025 (slide 63), and Oil & Gas accounted for $740 million. On the other hand, VIST had revenues of $2.47 billion, of which $2.38 billion was for crude oil sales.

As I explained in my previous article, Pampa chose approximately three years ago to get into the business of pure crude oil with the purchase of the Rincón de Aranda field, located in the Vaca Muerta formation and wholly owned by the company. This move positioned Pampa as a competitor to Argentina's major oil producers, such as YPF and VIST. But Pampa's core was (and still is) gas.

The company's total oil production is 81,200 barrels of oil equivalent per day (kboepd), of which only 18,000 kboepd are sold as crude oil, approximately 23%. On the other hand, the rest of the production belongs to 11 million cubic meters per day. Translated into crude oil measurements, this equates to between 64 and 65 kboepd. To sum up: 77% of Pampa's oil production is used for gas production.

This is also something I saw in the four oil and gas blocks controlled by the company. Only Rincón de Aranda is a pure oil block, while the others (Sierra Chata, Río Neuquén, and El Mangrullo) have gas infrastructure. In terms of profitability, I clearly see pure oil production is giving a really interesting EBITDA. From $375 million EBITDA in E&P (Exploration and Production), $126 million corresponds to oil.

However, when I incorporated this operating segment into the total, the oil margins were lower than I had anticipated. In summary, E&P represented 35% of the Adjusted EBITDA in 2025, but the weight of gas is bigger, so pure oil EBITDA is between 11% and 13% of the total. The rest of the crude market corresponds to gas distribution, which is handled by Transportadora de Gas del Sur S.A. (TGS), a company co-controlled by Pampa. TGS is a key part of Pampa's midstream business, which only underscores the gas focus. The other half of Pampa's margins comes from electricity generation. 44% of Adjusted EBITDA comes from generation (Slide 7 of the Investor Presentation).

Although the pure crude oil business of Pampa has very good margins, it still represents a small portion of total EBITDA. Meanwhile, the company accounts for it within the Oil & Gas segment. In 2025, this segment represented a $91 million EBIT, a better result compared to 2024, which was $61 million. However, EBIT was $503 million, so Oil & Gas represented 18%. The largest part of the EBIT increase came from electricity generation, which rose from $204 million to $375 million. In summary, electricity generation represented almost 75% of EBIT in 2025. So, electric generation seems to be the core of Pampa, generating higher sales revenue, higher EBIT, and the best EBITDA. The Utilities segment, managed by TGS and Transener, represents 15% of EBITDA.

Electricity Generation is the Core of Pampa, but Not Its Main Catalyst

The issue with electricity generation is that I believe it lacks strong long-term catalysts beyond natural demand growth and the deregulation of certain sectors. Even more: 79% of EBITDA comes from the regulated segment managed by the state-owned company CAMMESA.

If CAMMESA reduces its participation in the Argentine market as a central regulator, PAMPA could achieve better clients at better prices. The tariff catalysts, given that the electricity sector is highly regulated, were already priced in Pampa since Javier Milei's arrival to the presidency of Argentina in 2023.

The free market reforms made by the Argentine government to raise service prices caused companies like Pampa, whose business is largely focused on the electricity market, to benefit. In the last three years, Pampa's shares rose almost 140%. At the same time, Argentina's macroeconomic stability boosted the company's investments, which totaled $1.2 billion in 2025. But of that CAPEX, $1 billion was dedicated to oil and gas.

So, what I see in the generation sector of Pampa, the more stable sector from my point of view and with the greatest strength to generate cash flow, is that today it lacks clear catalysts. With the exception of privatizations or further deregulation measures in the electricity market. Another catalyst could be increasing the market share in generation, which is currently 15%, but I believe these possibilities are already priced into the market.

Outlook for Q1 2026 Earnings

Given this outlook for the 2025 results, which I consider generally positive, Pampa will present its Q1 2026 earnings on May 6th. I believe it is very important to look carefully at the income of the Oil & Gas segment, and therefore I also believe we need to understand the data I showed you above.

I took a closer look at crude oil production. In 2025, daily barrel production rose 145%, while the average international barrel price went from $70 in 2024 to $61 in 2025, a 12% decrease. The production increase, based at the Rincón de Aranda field, allowed Pampa to achieve sales of $862 million, 18% higher than in 2024. But as I mentioned before, the segment's EBIT was only $91 million. Even more: the segment reported a total loss of $55 million due to financing costs.

My first impression of the oil environment is that Pampa could report a strong increase in revenue in Oil & Gas. The average WTI price in Q1 2026 was $71, representing an increase of nearly 18% compared to the $60 average recorded in the previous quarter. Although this increase may not represent the bulk of Oil & Gas revenue, as explained above, it will imply better reported margins and could point to a more positive outlook for 2026.

The market expects revenue of $473 million for the quarter, below the $504 million reported in the previous quarter. However, if this estimate is met, it would represent an increase of approximately 21% YoY. I also expect to see more details on expansion plans in the oil sector in the context of the conflict in the Middle East, supply cuts, and price volatility. As a guide, the company already outlined a production plan for 2029 in its 2025 annual report.

Valuation

I believe the stock price has long-term upside potential thanks to investments in Rincón de Aranda. The goal is to reach 45,000 barrels of oil per day in 2027, increasingly gaining ground in the crude oil business and capitalizing on the Vaca Muerta boom. The other segments of Pampa also excite me, and it's a stock I would hold for a long time.

However, as in my January article, the current price forces us to pay a premium for the company's potential. The EV/EBITDA multiple of 8x remains relatively in line with the 5-year average (8.7x). At the same time, the stock is trading at 7.7 times its expected earnings, while the 5-year average is also 7.5 times expected earnings.

But as I believe Pampa is still undervalued (I mean, its multiples could be even higher), I think it's time to upgrade my rating from Hold to Buy. Analyst estimates indicate an average target price of $112, which would imply a 40% increase. The lowest estimate is a target price of $90, an increase of approximately 20%. In other words, even in a more challenging environment, the company's weak performance so far in 2026, I believe, gives us a buying signal.

I don't see new catalysts beyond the ones that I analyzed before. But I do see the stock has been left behind compared to other energy companies. So, for anyone who has Pampa in their portfolio, I think it's time to increase their position.

Risks: Weak Momentum in the Argentine Market

In my previous investment thesis on Pampa, I discussed the risks of the Argentine market, its political uncertainty, and its economic volatility. I also mentioned the economic reforms that needed to be passed in Congress to make the labor market more flexible and offer tax incentives for investment, among other issues.

The main economic debates resulted in a victory for the ruling party. The labor reform suffered a minor legal setback, but the Argentine government expects to overcome it in the short term. So, to my surprise, news of the reforms' approval failed to boost Argentine equities, keeping the market almost at the same levels as after Milei's electoral victory in October 2025. I'm using the Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (ARGT) to illustrate this point.

From last October until now, the Argentine market has remained at an average price of $93-$94. This weak momentum in Argentine equities in 2026 affects all stocks in the country, including Pampa.

Frankly, I cannot explain with much certainty why all of this is happening. Analysts say that credit concerns and inflation, the persistence of capital controls (currency restrictions), and the shift of funds from equities to fixed income (sovereign bonds) are some of the determining factors behind the loss of momentum. Personally, I believe that MSCI's standalone rating remains the main obstacle for the Argentine market, limiting capital inflows. Despite all of this, the Argentine electricity sector is still favored by major firms, such as Morgan Stanley.

Conclusions

I believe Pampa Energía is a very attractive option to participate in the electric power development of Argentina, despite the political and economic challenges. I also highlighted the natural risk of making an investment in foreign markets. Argentina is not going through its best moment in its benchmark ETF that I used, ARGT.

I also couldn't find new catalysts for the company. In my previous article I also mentioned the main reasons to trust the company, and I recommended a Hold rating because the stock was trading at prices that already reflected its potential. Now, three months later and very close to the earnings presentation, I am upgrading my rating to Buy. Given that the company is achieving the best margins in the crude oil segment, in the end, the rise in oil prices could benefit Rincón de Aranda's profitability in the short term.

Given its leading position in the country's electricity generation market, I believe Pampa now deserves a chance, with analysts forecasting target prices near $90, even at the lower end of the range. So, I upgrade my rating on Pampa from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

The Sustainable Value Strategy underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Value Index, in the first quarter.

Strategy Additions & Sales

New Positions

During the quarter, we initiated five positions: Leggett & Platt, Inc., Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc., Methanex, Dick's Sporting Goods, and Jack Henry & Associates.

Leggett & Platt, Inc. (LEG) is a diversified manufacturer of engineered components for bedding, furniture, and automotive markets. We'd wager that at least one bed in your home contains Leggett components — at 50% market share, the odds are squarely in our favor.

With a veteran CEO returning, the company recently completed a major restructuring expected to generate meaningful annual cost savings, while deleveraging through asset sales and a reduced dividend. The stock has sold off to multi-decade lows as cyclical weakness across its end markets has pressured earnings.

LEG trades at less than 10x 2026 estimated earnings vs. historically trading at over 17x. We are confident in the underlying business given its durable market leadership, vertically integrated supply chain, strong free cash flow generation (it trades at a double-digit free cash flow yield), and potential positive turn in the mattress industry. Furthermore, the recent unsolicited takeover bid from Somnigroup underscores the significant value not reflected in the current stock price.

Sprouts Farmers Market, Inc. (SFM) is a specialty grocery retailer focused on fresh, natural, and organic foods. Its unique health-focused stores built around its farm-stand heritage differentiate it from conventional grocers and position it to capitalize on the secular consumer shift toward better-for-you food. With stores in 25 states nationwide, the company is executing a long-term ~10% store growth algorithm, including into underpenetrated markets across the Northeast and Midwest.

The stock retreated meaningfully from its 2025 peak as comparable store sales decelerated due to macro-driven consumer caution. We are confident in the underlying business given its best-in-class gross margins, curated product selection driving customer loyalty, and improving fundamentals through private label expansion and supply chain self-distribution. We like that it trades at a below-market multiple of 13.9x 2026 estimated earnings, but still is on track to continue its double-digit earnings growth algorithm.

Methanex (MEOH) is the world's largest producer and distributor of methanol. We believe the stock is well positioned to benefit from a tightening global supply-demand backdrop, particularly in light of escalating conflict in the Middle East. The company's global production footprint and integrated logistics network provide a structural advantage in supplying customers during periods of disruption.

Recent geopolitical developments—specifically reduced Iranian supply and broader regional instability—are already contributing to higher methanol pricing. Importantly, this dynamic is occurring against a backdrop of structurally constrained supply and steady demand growth across industrial and energy-related applications, suggesting pricing strength may be more durable than typical cyclical spikes. It trades at 10.7x estimated 2026 earnings.

From a responsible business perspective, we believe that Methanex stands out within the chemicals sector. It has industry-leading safety performance, no major environmental incidents in recent years, and meaningful progress on emissions intensity reduction ahead of stated targets. The company is also actively investing in lower-carbon methanol pathways, including biomethanol and carbon capture initiatives, positioning it to participate in longer-term decarbonization trends such as marine fuel transition.

Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS) presents what we see to be a compelling investment case driven by a combination of cyclical tailwinds and company-specific execution. On the demand side, higher tax refunds relative to last year should provide incremental support to discretionary spending, particularly in categories such as athletic apparel, footwear, and equipment, where DKS has strong market positioning. This is further amplified by the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup in the United States, which is expected to drive increased participation and consumer interest in soccer and related sporting categories, creating a multi-quarter demand tailwind. It trades at 14.9x estimated 2026 earnings.

Importantly, DKS is also well positioned to benefit from improving industry dynamics, including a potential turnaround at Foot Locker in the second half of 2026. This could help stabilize key vendor relationships—most notably with Nike—and lead to a more rational promotional environment across athletic retail. While near-term consumer headwinds remain, we believe DKS's differentiated merchandising strategy, premium store experience, and strong relationship with youth sports position the company to capture outsized share as conditions normalize, supporting continued margin resilience and long-term earnings growth.

Jack Henry & Associates (JKHY) represents what we consider to be a high-quality compounder that we find to be recently more attractive following a valuation reset driven by broad-based concerns around AI disruption in financial technology. JKHY provides backend technology for small and medium sized banks. We believe the sell-off has been largely indiscriminate, creating a rare opportunity to acquire a mission-critical, deeply entrenched business at a meaningful discount to its historical valuation. We also believe their largest competitor will force RFPs to Jack Henry as they are consolidating platforms which can cause havoc for customers. This could create a very compelling opportunity to take market share.

JKHY's competitive moat remains intact, underpinned by several durable advantages: a highly regulated end market, long-standing customer relationships with community and regional banks, and deep integration within clients' core processing and digital infrastructure. These systems are not easily displaced, given high switching costs, regulatory complexity, and the operational risk associated with transitioning core banking platforms.

While the market has questioned the long-term impact of AI on traditional fintech providers, we view JKHY as a beneficiary rather than a casualty, given its role as a trusted technology partner and data steward within the banking ecosystem. With strong recurring revenue, high retention rates, and consistent free cash flow generation, JKHY offers a compelling combination of resilience and long-term growth, particularly at current valuation levels which we believe understate the durability of its business model.

Exits

We exited several positions during the quarter, reflecting our ongoing discipline around valuation, portfolio fit, and risk management.

We sold our position in Amdocs (DOX) as we chose to redeploy capital toward names with more attractive upside.

Our exit from Ciena (CIEN) came early in the quarter. The market cap blew past $50 billion (it is now $66 billion) and was added to the S&P 500. Ideally every company we sell would be for this reason.

Federal Agricultural Mortgage (AGM) was sold in several tranches through the quarter. The agricultural lending environment has remained stable, but we felt there is more upside in JKHY.

We closed our position in Lazard (LAZ). With the M&A cycle showing potential signs of slowing and two record-breaking IPOs stealing share, we felt the stock was pricing in a more optimistic capital markets environment than we were prepared to underwrite.

Our position in Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL) was liquidated in early March. The bank had been a strong holding for us, but we became nervous with their large loan balances to non depository financial institutions and credit issues with First Brands.

Disclosures: The information contained herein represents the opinion of Riverwater and should not be construed as personalized or individualized investment advice. Analysis and opinion expressed in this report are subject to change without notice. Reader should not assume that investments in the securities identified were or will be profitable. Timing differences of purchases and sales may have a modest impact on the actual contribution numbers presented. The holdings identified do not represent all the securities purchased, sold, or recommended. The calculation's methodology along with details on all holding's contribution to the overall account's performance during the measurement period are available upon request. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

The contributions presented are shown on a gross basis and do not reflect the deduction of management fees, or other fees. The deduction of management fees reduces investment returns and would also affect the investment characteristics presented. Each contributor's share of performance is shown for informational purposes only and is not predictive of future performance or investment outcomes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Coherent: A Buy Driven By Robust AI Datacenter Optics Demand
data.description:
Coherent (COHR) is positioned at the intersection of three technology pillars underpinning the AI infrastructure buildout—compound semiconductors, photonics, and optical networking. The company was able to execute a sharp turnaround following a difficult downcycle in FY2023-FY2024, which saw challenges integrating after the II-VI/Coherent merger and inventory corrections. Revenue has rebounded back to $5.8B in FY2025 and is expected to reach $6.95B/$8.78B in FY2026E/FY2027E with growth rates of 19.6%/26.3% Y/Y, respectively. Critically, EPS is expected to compound at an even faster rate at a 3-year CAGR of ~45% from FY2025 to FY2028E.

The stock trades at an FY2027E P/E of 44.5x and a PEG ratio of 1.45x, which is not too expensive in the current market and its growth rate. It also boasts a defensible moat in its proprietary manufacturing of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and its emerging 1.6T/co-packaged optics franchise.

Investment thesis

1. The core engine for AI data center optics

AI hyperscalers deploy GPU clusters that need dense, high-bandwidth optical interconnections between each compute node at scale. Coherent has been a leading independent supplier of INP-based transceivers for 400G, 800G, and now 1.6T applications. As such, the management has reported record bookings that extend well into FY2027E with an impressive 4x book-to-bill for 1.6T.

The transition to 6-inch InP wafers has become the key supply-side differentiator. This technology delivers four times the chip count at less than half of the cost of the legacy 3-inch process. The production ramp began in August 2025, and Coherent targets a doubling of total InP capacity within FY2026E. This creates a structural cost wedge against competitors. At OFC 2026, the company demonstrated its 3.2T and 12.8T transceiver architectures, reassuring the longevity and visibility of Coherent's technological roadmap, which extends well beyond this cycle.

The market expects FY2026E to grow 19.6% Y/Y to $6.95B, driven primarily by the data center & communications segment. Revenue is projected to further accelerate to $8.78B in FY2027E, reflecting a 26.3% Y/Y increase. This is already partially supported by its record bookings.

2. Structural buildup in operating leverage

The three forces driving margin expansion are due to 1) the 6-inch InP wafer transition reducing unit manufacturing cost, 2) the mix shifting towards higher speed/ASP 800G and 1.6T transceivers that carry higher margins than legacy 100G/400G, and 3) a relatively fixed SG&A and R&D cost base. According to the Q2 FY2026 earnings call, CFO Sherri Luther has suggested a long-term adjusted gross margin that will exceed 42%, while market consensus estimates for the outer years lean more conservatively. Still, the trajectory is clear as margins have bottomed out in FY2024 and are moving in a positive direction.

Fiscal Year Adj. Gross Margin Adj. EBITDA Margin Adj. Net Income Margin
FY2024A 36.0% 21.3% 5.5%
FY2025A 37.9% 22.2% 9.7%
FY2026E 39.0% 23.6% 15.0%
FY2027E 40.4% 25.3% 17.4%
FY2028E 40.5% 28.6% 20.0%

Note: Coherent excludes 1) amortization of acquired tangibles, 2) stock-based compensation, and 3) integration/site consolidation charges in adjusted figures.

Source: Company, Market estimates, Himalayas Research estimates

3. Optionality in Silicon Carbide, yet de-risked

Reaching beyond optics, Coherent is a leading SiC substrate manufacturer for EV power inverters and AI data center power conversion systems. Denso and Mitsubishi Electric invested $1B in Coherent late 2023, which serves to validate the company's technology while also providing capital. A 300mm SiC platform was recently announced and will target AI data center thermal management in addition to EV and industrial electrification applications.

However, SiC revenue has been weak, with the Materials segment registering a 6% Y/Y drop in FY2025. We understand that this is a timing mismatch in EV ramp schedules rather than underlying softness. We believe that the market is discounting the optionality with the EV growth thesis, and because the Denso/Mitsubishi relationship provides a durable cushion, there could be asymmetric upside should EV adoption accelerates.

4. Emerging revenue streams from CPO, OCS, and Apple

Coherent has secured a large CPO (co-packaged optics) purchase, which will use its proprietary high-power CW lasers. The CPO architecture is expected to dominate next-generation AI switch designs at 3.2T and beyond and will replace pluggable modules with directly integrated laser sources to eliminate insertion loss.

The company is shipping optical circuit switch (OCS) systems to several customers. The management has doubled its prediction for its SAM from $2B to $4B (investor deck, pp. 21), fueled by broader use cases across scaling and spine applications, and faster-than-expected customer adoption.

Apple has entered into a multi-year laser supply agreement with the company and is expected to begin contributing revenue in H2 CY2026. This adds a diversified, first tier consumer electronics revenue stream to an otherwise hyperscaler-focused customer mix.

Balance sheet and capital expenditure

The company carries an improved debt-to-equity ratio of 0.41x with a net debt of $2.68B, having reduced total debt from $4.5B in FY2023 to $3.5B in its latest filing.

FCF was modest in FY2025 at $193M due to an elevated capex of $441M to fund the 6-inch InP and SiC capacity buildout. We expect FCF to accelerate during FY2027E to FY2028E as capex moderates and operating leverage improves. Market estimates put FY2027E FCF at $851M. Since the II-VI merger, management has opted for a more capital-light approach and remains focused on deleveraging.

Key Risks

Coherent has a high concentration of hyperscaler customers, so it could be subject to their capex cyclicality. So far, the AI capex cycle has bucked softer macro trends and remains robust.

There is intense competition from Lumentum (LITE), Marvell's (MRVL) potential in-house optics, and new entrants like POET, which will compete on transceiver pricing. Also, Nvidia could also vertically integrate into optics, creating a tail risk.

Automotive SiC demand is still below satisfaction, as we can see from the softness in the Materials segment. The timing with SiC lies on EV adoption, but is partially offset by partnership capital.

The company faces execution risks as it juggles scaling 6-inch InP, and 300mm SiC and building out CPO/OCS product lines.

Valuation and verdict

Coherent is a very compelling photonics ramp investment story. There is significant tailwind coming from non-discretionary optical infrastructure requirements of AI scaling. Revenue is expected to surge from $5.8B to $10.6B from FY2025 to FY2028E, or a CAGR of 22%, while EPS is set to grow even faster at a CAGR of 45%, thanks to expanding margins.

The stock is not trading too rich at ~45x FY27E P/E and an FY26E PEG ratio of 1.4x, especially if we consider the fast, compounding growth to be expected. The company has an InP manufacturing moat that no other pure-play photonics peer can replicate at scale.

We rate COHR a Buy.

data.title: Century Aluminum: A Decade-Long Growth Runway
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Riverwater Sustainable Value Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary.

Company Deep Dive: Century Aluminum Company (CENX)

CENX is the largest domestic producer of primary aluminum in the United States, operating smelters in Kentucky, South Carolina, and Iceland with combined annual production capacity of approximately 770,000 tonnes. Glencore plc is the largest shareholder at approximately 30% beneficial ownership 1 and also the company's largest customer (54% of 2025 sales). 2

U.S. Primary Aluminum

The U.S. went from producing 4.65 million tonnes of primary aluminum at 33 smelters in 1980 to approximately 670,000 tonnes at just 4 operating smelters today — a collapse from 33% of global output to under 1%. 3 The country now relies on imports for roughly 60% of its aluminum consumption. 4 Section 232 tariffs (currently 50% on imports) create a meaningful domestic price premium for U.S. producers. LME aluminum prices have risen above $3,300/ton 5 on tightening global supply, with Chinese production capped at 45 million tonnes and Gulf smelter capacity at risk from geopolitical disruption in the Strait of Hormuz — a dynamic we have termed the "Fog of Excursion" thesis.

Oklahoma Mega-Project

In January 2026, Century announced a landmark joint development agreement with Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) to build a 750,000-tonne-per-year primary aluminum smelter in Inola, Oklahoma — the first new U.S. smelter since 1980. Century will own 40% of the joint venture, supported by a $500 million DOE grant. 6 This single project would more than double current U.S. primary aluminum production and represents a decade-long growth runway. Construction is expected to begin late 2026, with first production by the end of the decade. 7

Financial Outlook: Century reported full-year 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $425.1 million on $2.53 billion of net sales. 8 Management guided Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $215–235 million, implying an annualized run rate approaching $900 million as higher LME pricing flows through on a lagged basis. 9 Near-term catalysts include the Mt. Holly capacity restart (50,000+ tonnes, targeting full production by June 2026), 10 Grundartangi Line 2 recovery to near-full output by July 2026, 11 and the Hawesville smelter monetization ($200 million cash plus a 6.8% equity stake in a TeraWulf-developed data center project). 12 Each $100/ton movement in LME aluminum has an estimated $60–70 million annualized EBITDA impact. 13 These projections reflect our internal estimates and are subject to change based on company execution and market conditions.

Valuation

At the time of purchase, CENX shares were trading at approximately 6.1x our 2026E EBITDA and roughly 2.1x trailing twelve-month sales. Metals & Mining comps typically trade at 4.0–6.0x EBITDA, but CENX arguably warrants a premium given the structural growth optionality from the Oklahoma mega-project — which at mid-cycle prices could contribute $300–400 million in proportional EBITDA by decade-end. 14 B. Riley recently raised its price target to $86. We believe the market is assigning minimal value to the Oklahoma project and the broader reindustrialization narrative.

Responsible Business

Century's Grundartangi smelter in Iceland runs on nearly 100% renewable energy (geothermal and hydroelectric), producing the company's "Natur-Al" branded low-carbon aluminum for the European market. The Oklahoma project's potential to secure clean energy from wind and solar sources could make it the cleanest large-scale smelter in U.S. history. Century's role in reducing American dependence on imported aluminum from higher-carbon foreign sources provides an additional sustainability dimension. We note that the company disclosed a material weakness in internal controls over financial reporting related to a 2023–2025 restatement, which is being actively remediated. 15
